items.q: How did Hitler come to power in 1933?
items.correct: He was legally appointed Chancellor by President Hindenburg as part of a conservative political deal, after the Nazis became the largest party in the Reichstag.
items.misconception: Hitler seized power through a violent coup or revolution.
items.random_wrong: Hitler won an outright majority in the 1932 presidential election.
items.why: The 1923 Beer Hall Putsch failed; in 1933 Hitler came to power constitutionally. Students confuse Nazi violence with the actual mechanism of takeover, which was legal manoeuvring.

items.q: What did Magna Carta actually do in 1215?
items.correct: It was a peace treaty between King John and rebel barons protecting baronial privileges and limiting arbitrary royal taxation and imprisonment — it was annulled within months.
items.misconception: Magna Carta gave ordinary English people democratic rights and freedom.
items.random_wrong: Magna Carta abolished the monarchy and created Parliament.
items.why: Magna Carta protected the elite, not commoners; villeins are barely mentioned. Its democratic reputation is a later (17th-century) reinterpretation projected backwards.

items.q: What was the political significance of the Reichstag Fire?
items.correct: Hitler used it to issue the Reichstag Fire Decree suspending civil liberties, jail Communists, and create the conditions for the Enabling Act of March 1933.
items.misconception: Hitler personally set the fire and blamed it on the Communists.
items.random_wrong: The fire destroyed all of Berlin and forced people to support the Nazis.
items.why: Historians still debate who started it; van der Lubbe likely acted alone. What matters is how Hitler exploited it — a key step in the legal slide to dictatorship.

items.q: Which Weimar Constitution article let the President rule by emergency decree?
items.why: Correct answer: Article 48. “Article 23” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Who was the Nazi Minister of Propaganda?
items.why: It's Goebbels. “Himmler” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many Reichstag seats did the Nazis hold after the July 1932 election?
items.why: Exact answer: 230. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
